does: This paper introduces a novel multi-turn jailbreaking attack against frontier language models that have adopted safe completion strategies. The method exploits a fundamental vulnerability in safety approaches that try to infer user intent: attackers can gradually establish false benign intent across conversation turns, causing the model to relax safety constraints while completing increasingly harmful requests. The core contribution is demonstrating that intent inversion—where an attacker pretends to have benign goals while actually pursuing harmful ones—is particularly effective in multi-turn settings where repeated interactions allow an attacker to reinforce deceptive framing and build apparent conversational trust before pivoting to malicious requests.
connects: This work builds on and responds to the evolution of safety training in large language models. Earlier approaches relied on binary refusal boundaries trained to distinguish safe from unsafe content based on perceived intent, but these proved brittle when attackers obfuscated intent, creating unhelpful systems. Recent frontier models shifted from refusal-based to safe completion approaches, which maximize helpfulness while maintaining safety constraints—attempting to overcome the brittleness problem. This paper connects to adversarial attack research on language models and the broader literature examining vulnerabilities in intent-based safety mechanisms, showing that the newer safe completion paradigm introduces new exploitable attack surfaces that differ from those targeting refusal-based systems.
enables: This work illuminates critical vulnerabilities in current safety paradigms and should motivate future research into more robust safety mechanisms. It points toward the need for safety approaches that go beyond intent inference alone—perhaps incorporating consistency checks across conversations, detecting patterns of deceptive intent shifts, or developing defenses specifically designed for multi-turn attack scenarios. The findings suggest that frontier models require fundamentally different safety strategies that account for the possibility of sustained deception rather than single-turn adversarial inputs. This research enables better threat modeling and could drive development of safety training methods that maintain both genuine helpfulness and resilience against sophisticated intent-deception attacks.
